title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people from different generations who reflect on how values and habits differ across generations. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Different Times: Four People on Generational Values and Habits

texts.A:
### Miriam, 74, Retired Social Worker
I grew up in a household where you repaired things rather than replaced them, saved before you spent, and expected nothing to come easily. [43]I sometimes find it genuinely difficult to understand the level of anxiety my grandchildren express about problems that my generation would simply have got on with – not because they are less resilient, but because the world sends them so many more competing messages about who they should be.[/43] I don't say this critically; I say it with real compassion, and some envy – they know themselves far better than I did at their age. [47]What I most admire about younger people is their willingness to name and discuss their emotional lives openly, which my generation entirely lacked the language for.[/47] [51]My granddaughter has taught me more about the world I'm living in than most books I've read, and I think the direction of learning between generations is often the reverse of what people assume.[/51]
texts.B:
### Dan, 44, Secondary School Teacher
[44]Teaching for twenty years has given me a front-row seat to generational change, and what strikes me most is the shift in young people's relationship with authority – students today expect to be given reasons rather than simply instructions, which I find refreshing rather than difficult.[/44] My own teachers rarely explained why we had to do anything; compliance was simply expected. [48]I do worry about the effect of social media on my students' capacity to concentrate on a single task for an extended period – not because they are unintelligent, but because their attention is being commercially harvested in ways they cannot fully see or resist.[/48] [52]The most encouraging thing I observe is how politically and socially aware young people are today – the idea that they are apathetic, I think, confuses a different style of engagement with no engagement at all.[/52]
texts.C:
### Isabelle, 31, Product Manager
[45]Being in my early thirties means I'm old enough to remember life before smartphones but young enough to be entirely fluent in digital culture – I sometimes feel like a translator between two worlds that consistently talk past each other.[/45] I see my parents' generation dismiss online relationships as shallow, and my younger colleagues dismiss phone calls as invasive. Both judgements strike me as too sweeping. [49]What I value about my generation's attitude is the rejection of the idea that a stable career at a single company for forty years defines professional success – the flexibility my peers pursue is something earlier generations had little choice but to forego.[/49]
texts.D:
### Ethan, 19, A-level Student
[46]People of my parents' age often tell me I don't know how fortunate I am, and I understand why they say it, but I think they sometimes underestimate how different the pressures are now – everything we do is permanently documented and potentially public in a way that is genuinely exhausting.[/46] The mistakes I make don't disappear the way theirs did. [50]I think my generation is more pragmatic and less ideologically tribal than older people expect – we've grown up watching institutions fail, and most people I know are better at distinguishing what actually matters from what is just noise.[/50] I'm less interested in labels and more interested in outcomes.

questions.question: expresses sympathy, rather than criticism, for the difficulties facing a younger generation?
questions.explanation: Miriam says she cannot understand her grandchildren's anxiety, but says this with compassion and some envy – not criticism – noting the world sends them far more competing messages.

questions.question: describes noticing a specific shift in young people's relationship with authority over many years of professional experience?
questions.explanation: Dan says teaching for twenty years has shown him that students now expect reasons rather than instructions, which he finds refreshing.

questions.question: describes themselves as positioned between two generations and capable of understanding both perspectives?
questions.explanation: Isabelle says she is old enough to remember life before smartphones but young enough to be fluent in digital culture, feeling like a translator between two worlds.

questions.question: challenges the assumption that their generation has it easier or more fortunately than those before them?
questions.explanation: Ethan says he understands why older people say he doesn't know how fortunate he is, but argues they underestimate how different and exhausting modern pressures are.

questions.question: mentions a specific aspect of younger people's behaviour that they genuinely admire?
questions.explanation: Miriam says she most admires young people's willingness to discuss their emotional lives openly, something her own generation lacked the language for.

questions.question: expresses concern about a specific technology's effect on a cognitive ability in young people?
questions.explanation: Dan worries about social media's effect on students' capacity for sustained concentration, saying their attention is commercially harvested in ways they can't fully resist.

questions.question: describes a change in attitude towards careers and professional life that they see as positive?
questions.explanation: Isabelle values her generation's rejection of the single-employer career model and says the flexibility they pursue is something earlier generations couldn't choose.

questions.question: describes their own generation as more practically minded and less politically tribal than others expect?
questions.explanation: Ethan says his generation is more pragmatic and less ideologically tribal than older people expect, having grown up watching institutions fail.

questions.question: acknowledges learning from a younger person in a direction that is typically assumed to be the other way?
questions.explanation: Miriam says her granddaughter has taught her more about the world than most books, and thinks the direction of learning between generations is often the reverse of what people assume.

questions.question: challenges a widespread negative perception of young people's level of engagement with social or political issues?
questions.explanation: Dan says the idea that young people are apathetic confuses a different style of engagement with no engagement at all, and he finds their awareness encouraging.
